title: Time Series Momentum
abstract: We document significant time series momentum in equity index, currency, commodity, and bond futures for each of the 58 liquid instruments we consider. We find persistence in returns for one to 12 months that partially reverses over longer horizons, consistent with sentiment theories of initial under-reaction and delayed over-reaction. A diversified portfolio of time series momentum strategies across all asset classes delivers substantial abnormal returns with little exposure to standard asset pricing factors and performs best during extreme markets.
citation: Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012), Time Series Momentum, Journal of Financial Economics, SSRN 2089463
license: Author working paper; abstract reproduced under fair use. Confirm full-text rights before redistributing.
notes: Hypothesis: scale each instrument to constant ex-ante vol, go long past 12m winners / short losers, rebalance monthly. Test on QC futures universe.

title: Pairs Trading: Performance of a Relative-Value Arbitrage Rule
abstract: We test a Wall Street investment strategy, pairs trading, with daily data over 1962-2002. Stocks are matched into pairs with minimum distance between normalized historical prices. A simple trading rule yields average annualized excess returns of up to 11 percent for self-financing portfolios of pairs. The profits are uncorrelated with the S&P 500 and are robust to conservative estimates of transaction costs. They are not explained by simple mean reversion as documented in the systematic profits to contrarian strategies.
citation: Gatev, Goetzmann & Rouwenhorst (2006), Pairs Trading, Review of Financial Studies, SSRN 141615
license: Abstract reproduced under fair use; confirm full-text rights before redistributing.
notes: Hypothesis: form pairs by minimum sum-of-squared-deviation over 12m, open at 2-sigma divergence, close at convergence. Watch transaction-cost sensitivity.

title: The Cross-Section of Expected Stock Returns
abstract: Two easily measured variables, size and book-to-market equity, combine to capture the cross-sectional variation in average stock returns associated with market beta, size, leverage, book-to-market equity, and earnings-price ratios. Moreover, when the tests allow for variation in beta that is unrelated to size, the relation between market beta and average return is flat, even when beta is the only explanatory variable.
citation: Fama & French (1992), The Cross-Section of Expected Stock Returns, Journal of Finance, SSRN 227930
license: Abstract reproduced under fair use; confirm full-text rights before redistributing.
notes: Hypothesis: build size and value factor sorts; use as cross-sectional ranking signal. Foundation for multi-factor screens in the Feature Agent.

title: Volatility Risk Premia and Expected Returns
abstract: The variance risk premium is the difference between the risk-neutral and physical expectation of future return variance. We show that selling variance (e.g., short straddles or variance swaps) earns a large, statistically significant premium that compensates for crash risk. The premium is time-varying, spikes during market stress, and is only partially explained by exposure to market returns, suggesting a distinct volatility risk factor.
citation: Eraker (2009), Volatility Risk Premia and Expected Returns, SSRN 1364697
license: Working paper; abstract reproduced under fair use.
notes: Hypothesis: harvest variance risk premium by systematically shorting variance, size by gap between implied and realized vol, hedge tail with deep OTM puts. Crash-risk sizing is critical.

title: The Probability of Backtest Overfitting
abstract: Many investment firms and portfolio managers rely on backtests to select investment strategies. Because computing power makes it easy to try thousands of model configurations, the strategy with the best in-sample performance is often overfit and fails out-of-sample. We introduce the Probability of Backtest Overfitting (PBO) via combinatorially symmetric cross-validation, and the Deflated Sharpe Ratio, to quantify how much a reported Sharpe ratio should be discounted given the number of trials.
citation: Bailey, Borwein, Lopez de Prado & Zhu (2015), The Probability of Backtest Overfitting, Journal of Computational Finance, SSRN 2326253
license: Abstract reproduced under fair use; confirm full-text rights before redistributing.
notes: Grounding for the Risk auditor: deflate reported Sharpe by trial count, compute PBO via CSCV. Ties directly to orchestrator deflated-Sharpe and the data-snooping ledger (docs/08).
